Tweet by @ericosiu (ericosiu)
Author bio: Founder - revenue agents @ singlebrain, ad agency @singlegrain, Investor. Member: @YPO Beverly Hills Podcaster: Marketing School, Leveling Up
Text: https://t.co/4gv3J1ImTu
Created: 2026-05-27T16:58:38.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":7,"reply_count":6,"like_count":88,"quote_count":4,"bookmark_count":204,"impression_count":28981}
Article content: {"title":"How to Stop Babysitting Your AI Agents","cover_media":"3_2056510001946951680","plain_text":"If an AI agent can't prove the work is done, your team still has to manage the work.

I keep seeing agent takes go viral: agents will replace workflows, talk to other agents, become the new employees, and change software.

I agree with the direction. But after putting agents into real marketing, sales, content, analytics, and ops workflows at Single Grain, I've become a lot less impressed by output and a lot more obsessed with proof.

The failure isn't that agents can't create things. The failure is that they say “done” too early.
Here's why.
The expensive version of “done”
Agent failure usually doesn't look dramatic.

It looks like this:

- “I drafted it” but the links are broken
- “I researched it” but the source is stale
- “I posted it” but the formatting is wrong
- “I updated the sheet” but the wrong tab changed
- “I finished the task” but nobody can tell what changed
- “I ran the workflow” but the output never reached the person who needed it

The demo looks magical. The handoff creates review debt.

That's the part most teams miss.

A bad agent doesn't always fail loudly. Sometimes it creates a polished draft that still needs a human to check the links, facts, formatting, source material, owner, next step, and delivery path.

You didn't remove labor. You moved it into audit work.
That audit work is where agent programs get expensive.

A human still has to open the file, check the source, click the links, compare the output against the brief, fix the formatting, decide who owns the next step, and explain the mistake when something gets missed.

That work doesn't show up in the agent demo.

But it shows up in the manager's calendar.

This is why some teams feel like they're "using AI everywhere" but don't feel meaningfully faster. 
The work moved from creation to inspection. The company got more output, but the trust layer didn't improve at all.
Output isn't completion
Here's the weak version:
 
That's still a manager babysitting an intern.

The useful version looks different:
 
That's what closed-loop work actually means.

A loop doesn't close because an agent completed a step. A loop closes when the system can answer five questions without a human detective:

1. What changed?
2. Where's the output?
3. Was it verified?
4. Who needs to review it?
5. What should happen next time?

Without that, you've got a confident intern with no manager.
The trust bottleneck
Companies are buying more agent tools before defining what “done” means.

Another agent platform. Another workflow builder. Another prompt library. Another Slack bot. Another meeting recorder. Another dashboard.

Then the business still doesn't feel faster.

The bottleneck isn't agent supply. The bottleneck is trust.

If I can't trust the output, I have to inspect it. If I have to inspect every output, the agent didn't save time. It created review debt with better branding.

That's why every agent workflow needs a Definition of Done.

Not a vibe. An actual checklist.

Steal this:
 
This looks obvious. But that's the point.
Most teams skip this because it feels too basic.

They want the more advanced questions:

Which model?
Which framework?
Which orchestration layer?
Which agent platform?
Which multi-agent setup?

Those questions matter eventually.

But if the agent can't tell you what changed, where the output went, and whether the work passed basic checks, the model choice isn't the bottleneck.

The boring checklist is what lets the workflow scale.

Every reliable operating system has boring standards underneath it. Naming conventions. QA rules. Review paths. Error logs. Owners. Handoffs.

Agents don't remove the need for that layer.

They make it EVEN more important.

The highest-ROI agent work is usually boring. The sexy part is the draft, report, analysis, outreach list, content brief, dashboard, or campaign plan.

The useful part is the proof trail.
What this looks like in real workflows

A newsletter agent isn't done when the copy exists.
It's done when the subject line is filled, the subtitle is filled, the links work, the source claims match the transcript, the sections are formatted correctly, the CTA points to the right place, the draft is in the right system, and the reviewer knows what changed.

A sales research agent isn't done when it gives you a list.

It's done when duplicates are removed, bad-fit accounts are filtered out, source URLs are included, ICP fit is scored, next-step messaging is drafted, the CRM or sheet is updated, and edge cases are flagged for human review.

A reporting agent isn't done when it prints numbers.

It's done when the numbers tie to the right source, the date range is clear, anomalies are explained, the business decision is stated, and the owner knows what to do next.

That's the standard most teams are missing.

They treat agents like output machines. The better mental model is QA-managed labor.

The agent should do the work AND carry its own proof.
Receipts beat confidence

Here's the operating table I use:
 
The last column is where trust comes from. Receipts beat confidence every time.

This also changes how you manage people who manage agents. The old question was: “How many tasks did you finish?”

The new question is: “How many verified outcomes did your system produce?”

Different scoreboard.
 
If your agent program can't track rework, exception rate, and review burden, you're probably measuring noise.

A team can generate 100 AI tasks and create zero business velocity. A team can generate 10 verified outcomes and move the company.

That's why some companies will get real value from agents while others create more Slack chaos.

The winners won't be the teams with the most agents.

The winners will be the teams with the clearest completion standards.
The one-page test
My rule:

Before adding another agent, write the Definition of Done for the current workflow.

Pick one workflow.

Content brief.  
Sales research.  
Weekly reporting.  
Call summary.  
Landing page QA.  
CRM cleanup.  
SEO refresh.  
Customer onboarding.

Then define done in one page.
 
If you can’t fill that out, the workflow isn’t ready for an agent.

It’s ready for clarification.

That’s less exciting than launching another tool.
But it works.

The biggest agent opportunity in most companies isn’t more autonomy.
It’s reducing the amount of human re-checking required before work can be trusted.

That’s where the margin is.
That’s where speed compounds.

That’s where agents become part of the operating system instead of another toy in the stack.

A good agent doesn’t just finish the task.

A good agent proves the work is finished, leaves the receipt, and makes the next run easier.
 
If you're a business interested in having AI systems built, you can go to https://www.singlebrain.com or for marketing help, just go to https://www.singlegrain.com
For more like this, level up your marketing with 14,000+ marketers and founders in my Leveling Up newsletter here for free: https://levelingup.beehiiv.com/subscribe
